title: Syzygium acrophilum
intro: Syzygium acrophilum, also known as the high-altitude water apple, is a plant species belonging to the family Myrtaceae. This plant has been found to possess various health benefits, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, which may help in reducing the risk of chronic diseases such as cancer and cardiovascular disease. The bioactive constituents of Syzygium acrophilum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Syzygium acrophilum is commonly used in traditional medicine and is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, as well as being incorporated into various herbal supplements and tonics.
taxonomy: Syzygium acrophilum is classified in the family Myrtaceae, which is a large and diverse family of flowering plants commonly known as myrtles. This family includes over 150 genera and more than 6,000 species worldwide. Syzygium acrophilum is a species of flowering plant native to the Philippines, and its classification within the family Myrtaceae is further supported by its characteristics such as having opposite leaves and small, white flowers. The genus Syzygium itself is a group of flowering plants that are primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions of Asia and Australia. The classification of Syzygium acrophilum within the family Myrtaceae is based on its morphological and molecular characteristics.
uses: Syzygium acrophilum is a species of tree that has been found to have various medicinal uses. Its bark and leaves have been traditionally used in folk medicine in the Philippines to treat various ailments. The tree is said to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which contribute to its medicinal uses. The bark is also used to treat fever and rheumatism, while the leaves are used to treat skin conditions. Further research is needed to fully understand the potential benefits and applications of Syzygium acrophilum's medicinal properties.

These constituents include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and essential oils, which have been identified in different parts of the plant.
